# agent-coherence

> agent-coherence is an open-source coherence protocol for shared artifacts in multi-agent LLM systems. It detects stale reads — moments when one agent's view of a shared artifact is older than another agent's just-committed version — and serves the current version on the next read instead of rebroadcasting the full artifact every turn. Implements a MESI-style cache-coherence protocol adapted from CPU cache design.
>
> Ships as **two products** with one underlying engine:
>
> 1. **A Python library** (`agent-coherence`) with drop-in adapters for LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen, and custom orchestrators. Vendor-neutral across model providers.
> 2. **A Claude Code plugin** (`agent-coherence-plugin`) that wires the coherence protocol into Claude Code's hook system. Detects when one session's view of `CLAUDE.md`, `plan.md`, `spec.md`, `runbook.md`, or other prose-shaped project rules drifts from another session's — or from what survives compaction.

## Positioning

The plugin's positioning one-liner (v0.1.1 marketplace listing, v0.1.1+ README, agent-coherence.dev/plugin): **"Coherence for the prose subset of project rules that can't be expressed as policy."**

This locates the plugin against the rules-as-prose vs rules-as-config structural framing:

| Class | Substrate | Right fix |
|---|---|---|
| Tool-class restrictions (e.g. `rg` over `grep`; no inline `python3 -c`) | `settings.json` `permissions.deny` — durable config | Migrate out of prose into config; survives subagent boundaries and compaction |
| Descriptive / architectural (`CLAUDE.md`, `plan.md`, `spec.md`, `runbook.md`, `DECISIONS.md`) | Prose | Can't be config-ified by definition. Versioning + coherence tracking. **This is where the plugin sits.** |

The two fixes don't compete. Moving config-able rules to `permissions.deny` is hygiene that reduces the surface the plugin has to track. The prose residue — design constraints, architectural decisions, active-work artifacts — is what's left for coherence tracking.

## The library — what it does

- **Detects stale reads.** When agent B reads an artifact after agent A has modified it, agent-coherence flags the stale read instead of silently serving outdated data. Most frameworks have no such mechanism; LangGraph's BaseStore documentation explicitly states it "does not natively support optimistic locking, vector versioning, or invalidation protocols."
- **Serves the current version on the next read.** Rather than broadcasting the full artifact to every agent on every turn (the default in CrewAI, AutoGen, and most LangGraph patterns), agent-coherence sends ~12-token invalidation signals when state changes and only re-fetches on the next read.
- **Enforces single-writer exclusivity per artifact.** Concurrent writes are prevented at the protocol level — not "resolved" by an append-only reducer that produces duplicates or unexpected list nesting.
- **Records what each agent saw.** Per-agent content-audit log with SHA-256 hashes per read, plus a sequence-numbered state-transition log. The data substrate for run replay and stale-read forensics.

## The plugin — what it does

Aligns with Anthropic's published guidance for project-rule enforcement in Claude Code (Aug 2025, restated 2026): *"For deterministic behavior, your best bet is to use hooks."* The plugin is the hook implementation that closes the artifact-coherence gap.

- **Wires `PreToolUse:Read` and `PreToolUse:Edit|Write` hooks** into Claude Code's hook system. On every `Read` of a tracked file, the hook checks coordinator state and injects an `additionalContext` warning if the artifact has been updated by another session.
- **Per-workspace SQLite-backed coordinator.** Survives compaction. Survives crash. Re-hydrates on next session start.
- **`permissions.deny`-aware.** Doesn't double-warn on tools the config already blocks.

## The mechanism — one log line in the agent's own context

When a tracked artifact's version has changed since this session last observed it, the `PreToolUse:Read` hook injects a single sentence into the agent's `additionalContext`: the path, the version this session last saw, the version the workspace coordinator currently holds, and who last wrote it. The model reads that sentence in line with the read it's about to perform.

One of two outcomes follows, and both appear in the session transcript:

- **The agent acknowledges and re-reads.** The next `Read` tool call fetches current content. The agent's response references the divergence explicitly. The user sees the trace.
- **The agent doesn't acknowledge.** The warning was emitted; the model chose not to act on it. That choice is in the transcript too — discoverable on review, attributable to the model, not silent.

The mechanism applies to anything important loaded at the session-start boundary — `CLAUDE.md`, `plan.md`, `spec.md`, `runbook.md`, `DECISIONS.md`, skill instructions, slash-command prompts, project rules. Anything loaded at a boundary that compaction crosses inherits the same shape.

## Verification outside the agent loop

Sub-agent `completed` is a control-flow signal, not a semantic one. When a sub-agent returns claiming "I wrote file X," the runtime knows it returned — it doesn't know whether the write happened, or whether the content is what the parent expected.

The plugin's coordinator gives you a second source. After a sub-agent returns, the workspace coordinator either saw a `PostToolUse:Edit` event on the claimed file from that session — or it didn't. `agent-coherence-status` surfaces the coordinator's view independent of any prose in the agent's final message. One primitive of the many a "completion with verification" pattern would need; queryable by any orchestrator before it accepts the sub-agent's claim and moves on.

## What the plugin doesn't do

Claiming less than the plugin does is the cheaper credibility trade.

- **v0.1 is warn-only.** The plugin surfaces drift via `additionalContext`. It does not block tool calls. Strict mode (`permissionDecision: "deny"`) is deferred to v0.2 pending a falsifiability experiment on Claude Code's retry-loop behavior.
- **It doesn't fix model-internal compaction drift.** When the model decides which prior context to retain across a compaction boundary, the plugin has no influence — that's an inference-layer decision.
- **It doesn't fix inference-layer corruption.** If the model produces incorrect output despite having current artifact content in context, that's a model-quality problem, not a coherence problem.
- **It doesn't fix sandbox or permissions gaps.** Tool-class restrictions belong in `permissions.deny`; the plugin tracks the prose residue that survives config-ification.

The plugin addresses the cross-session and cross-subagent artifact-coherence layer — one piece of the structural pattern Anthropic has explicitly chosen to leave to userspace.

## What the library fits in coding-agent workflows

agent-coherence is the coherence layer for any setup where multiple agents share mutable artifacts:

- **Subagent fleets reviewing the same codebase** — style + security + architecture + synthesizer reviewers on the same files, with cache hits on the second-through-Nth read instead of re-reading from scratch.
- **Planner-executor pairs editing a shared task spec** — the planner re-plans mid-flight (writes v2 with a 4th caller); the executor's behavior depends on whether write-side coherence is on.
- **Refactor graphs fanning out across worktrees** — multiple sub-agents touching the same files concurrently; the coordinator enforces single-writer ordering and invalidates stale caches before commits land.

## Measured impact (library)

### Simulation benchmarks

Reproducible on real LangGraph graphs in CI (uses `GenericFakeChatModel`; no live API calls):

| Workload | Reads:Writes | Cache hit rate | Token savings |
|---|---|---|---|
| Planning (read-heavy) | 12:1 | 75% | 69% |
| Code review (moderate) | 8:3 | 60% | 47% |
| High-churn (write-heavy) | 8:4 | 50% | 29% |

Reproduce: `make benchmark` after `pip install -e ".[langgraph,benchmark]"`.

### Real-LangGraph 4-agent code review

The repo ships `examples/shared_codebase/` as a working example: four reviewer subagents (style + security + architecture + synthesizer) read the same eight files in two passes each through `CCSStore`. Real LangGraph, deterministic fixture, no LLM API calls.

- 37.6% token reduction per run
- 16,820 tokens saved (44,702 → 27,882 per run)
- 35.3% cache hit rate over 51 read ops

Reproduce: `python -m examples.shared_codebase.main`.

### Recorded planner-executor refactor (three variants)

`examples/refactor_demo/` is a recorded demo showing two scripted sub-agents collaborating on a TypeScript refactor through `CCSStore`. The planner writes v1 of a task spec (rename 3 callers); the executor reads v1; the planner discovers a 4th caller and writes v2 mid-flight. Real `tsc --noEmit` against a real TypeScript fixture turns the correctness question into an on-screen build error.

Three variants assert the protocol mechanism + the application-level consequence:

- **`with`** — default `CCSStore`, lazy strategy. Executor reads v1 (cache SHARED), planner writes v2 (executor cache → INVALID), executor re-reads at commit → fetches v2. Op log: `[MISS][MISS][MISS][MISS]`. Outcome: `committed spec v2 (5 files renamed)`, `tsc: OK`.
- **`no-invalidation`** — `disable_invalidation(store)` patches the event bus' `publish_invalidation` to no-op. Executor cache stays SHARED at v1; commit-time get is `[HIT]` on stale v1. Op log: `[MISS][MISS][MISS][HIT]`. Outcome: `committed spec v1 (4 files)`, `tsc: FAIL TS2305` on `src/utils/session.ts`. **The protocol-level proof** — same code path, single-line bus suppression, different outcome.
- **`context-cache`** — executor never re-reads from the store; commits from the v1 spec it captured at read time. Mirrors LLM context-window behavior. Op log: `[MISS][MISS][MISS]` (only 3 ops; no commit-time read). Same `tsc: FAIL` outcome as `no-invalidation`. **The audience-facing demo** — same outcome as `no-invalidation`, with the mechanism (executor never re-reads) being what real LLM agents naturally exhibit in production.

Reproduce: `python -m examples.refactor_demo.main --variant=with|no-invalidation|context-cache`. See `outreach/demo/record.sh` in the website repo for the recording pipeline used on agent-coherence.dev/code.

## State-transitions visualization (the protocol in 4 beats)

The protocol's behavior on a single shared artifact, animated on agent-coherence.dev/code:

1. `t=0` — Planner writes v1 to the coordinator. Executor cache: empty.
2. `t=1` — Executor reads v1 from the coordinator. Executor cache: v1.
3. `t=2` — Planner writes v2. `CCSStore` publishes invalidation to peers before `write()` returns. Executor cache: INVALID.
4. `t=3` — Executor's next `get()` is MISS (cache invalidated); refetches v2. Executor cache: v2. `tsc` passes.

Single-writer ordering invariant: only one agent holds write permission on an artifact at a time. Monotonic versioning invariant: versions only increase. Both checked by `core/invariants.py` after every write/commit/invalidation.

## The trajectory framing (why this matters now)

Today's coding sub-agents run on **frozen specs**: the planner finishes, freezes a plan, then the executor runs. The two stages don't overlap, so coherence doesn't matter — the executor sees whatever the planner committed before exiting.

Tomorrow's coding sub-agents don't run that way. Deep Agents already does live re-planning. As soon as planners and executors run in parallel, frozen specs become stale specs, and stale specs become silent build failures. Coherence is what lets re-planning land in production without trampling the work already underway.

Pattern names showing up in production today: Claude Code Task subagent fleets, LangGraph code-review graphs with three or four reviewers on the same artifact, refactor pipelines fanning out planners and executors that edit a shared task spec. As of 2026, Anthropic's Agent View productized 10+ parallel Claude Code sessions in a single workspace — Research Preview, no documented state-isolation model.

## Install

**Library** (LangGraph / CrewAI / AutoGen / custom):

```
pip install "agent-coherence[langgraph]"
pip install "agent-coherence[crewai]"
pip install "agent-coherence[autogen]"
```

**Plugin** (Claude Code). Two steps — the Python coordinator + hook client come from PyPI; the Claude Code plugin metadata and hook wiring come from the marketplace:

```
# Step 1 — Python coordinator + hook client (PyPI 0.8.0a1)
pip install "agent-coherence>=0.8.0a1"

# Step 2 — Claude Code plugin
claude plugin marketplace add hipvlady/agent-coherence-plugin
claude plugin install agent-coherence@agent-coherence

# Track an artifact and observe state
agent-coherence-track docs/plans/feature-x.md
agent-coherence-status
```

**Status:** v0.1 private alpha · launch-gate verified on claude v2.1.131. The re-read behavior across consecutive validation runs hits the gate the v0.1 plan sets; the alpha cohort is being assembled by hand-pick rather than open sign-up. v0.1.1 ships to the public marketplace on a four-week timeline once the alpha cohort returns a clean signal.

## LangGraph drop-in (library)

```python
from ccs.adapters import CCSStore        # instead of langgraph.store.memory.InMemoryStore

store = CCSStore(strategy="lazy")
graph = builder.compile(store=store)
```

No node-code changes. Coherence happens at the store layer.

## Synchronization strategies

Five strategies ship out of the box. Choose by workload shape:

- `lazy` (default) — re-fetch on next read. Cheapest. Best for read-heavy workloads with infrequent writes.
- `eager` — push updates to all readers on write. Best for systems where every agent must always have the latest version.
- `lease` — TTL-based grants. Bounded staleness. Best for medium-churn workloads.
- `access_count` — invalidate after N reads since last write. Best for predictable read patterns.
- `broadcast` — replicate writes to all peers. Comparable to the framework default; useful as a baseline.

## Vendor neutrality

The protocol operates on artifacts and version metadata, not on model responses or tokens. Behavior is identical with:

- **Model providers:** Anthropic (Claude), OpenAI (GPT), Google (Gemini), Mistral, AWS Bedrock, Azure OpenAI, open-source models via Ollama / vLLM / llama.cpp.
- **Orchestration frameworks:** LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen, and custom orchestrators via `CoherenceAdapterCore`. Plus Claude Code via the plugin.

## The coordination invariant

Six issues on the public Claude Code tracker over three months across two surfaces share one underlying shape: **the runtime emits coordination signals that don't reflect actual state.** A capability or piece of state appears honored; the runtime can't honor it; the LLM completes the call as text rather than the system surfacing a hard failure.

Three places the same shape shows up:

1. **Rules-as-prose layer.** `CLAUDE.md` isn't in context after compaction; subagents don't inherit it on spawn.
2. **ACK layer.** A notification fires before the underlying process terminates; a claim doesn't wait for readiness.
3. **Artifact-state layer.** One session's view of a shared file doesn't reflect another session's update.

agent-coherence is the artifact-layer instance of this cross-cutting invariant: *signals about shared state must reflect actual state*. The plugin doesn't compete with the runtime fixes Anthropic is shipping for the other two layers — it sits at the layer those fixes don't reach: **concurrent multi-session state coherence across worktrees and across mid-session artifact edits.**

## TAM beyond CLAUDE.md

The same fragility applies to skill instructions and slash-command prompts loaded at session start. Anything important loaded at the session boundary — which is to say, anything loaded at a boundary that compaction crosses — has the same shape. `CLAUDE.md` is the visible case; every plugin that loads instructions at session start (slash commands, skill prompts, project rules) inherits it.

## Production safety

- **License:** Apache-2.0, public on GitHub. Both the library and the plugin.
- **Tests (library):** 165 unit + integration tests.
- **Formal verification:** Protocol safety properties (single-writer, monotonic versioning, no torn reads) model-checked with TLA+/TLC in CI.
- **Supply chain:** PyPI Trusted Publishers (OIDC), PEP 740 attestations, CycloneDX SBOM published with every release.
- **Crash recovery:** Optional sweep reclaims stale grants when agents OOM-kill or livelock (heartbeat-based, opt-in via `CrashRecoveryConfig`). The plugin's SQLite coordinator rehydrates after idle-shutdown.
- **Diagnose CLI:** `ccs-diagnose` is a zero-network static analyzer that detects stale-read risk in existing LangGraph graphs before adoption.

## Background reading

- `docs/why-coherence-matters.md` — public evidence of the consistency gap across LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen, and Claude Agent SDK, with linked sources.
- `docs/agent-coherence-approach.md` — how the MESI-derived approach maps to the documented gaps.
- arXiv 2603.15183 — formal protocol specification, simulation results, and proofs.
